summary: Microsoft's ADMX_Reliability policy page now closes the main contract gap for this record by mapping Enable Persistent Time Stamp directly to Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Reliability\TimeStampEnabled and documenting the enabled, disabled, and not-configured states. Decompiled OsEventsTimestampInterval still explains the companion TimeStampInterval cadence, the 24-hour cap, and the current-version fallback path, while the 25H2 Procmon and Ghidra follow-up keeps the current-build DiagTrack lead and adjacent Reliability\PBR runtime read in repo evidence.

setting.name: Reliability Event Timestamping
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can timestamp reliability events on a cadence. This setting controls whether that timestamping behavior runs for the machine.
setting.why_people_change_it: People only change it when debugging reliability reporting or comparing how often reliability events are timestamped.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling timestamping reduces reliability event detail. Leaving it enabled keeps the cadence available through the companion interval setting.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft's ADMX_Reliability policy page maps Enable Persistent Time Stamp directly to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Reliability\TimeStampEnabled and documents the enabled, disabled, and not-configured states. The decompiled OsEventsTimestampInterval path then explains the companion cadence behavior: it reads TimeStampEnabled first, returns 0 immediately when the value is 0, reads TimeStampInterval when the gate is non-zero, caps the policy-path interval at 86400 seconds, and falls back to HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Reliability\TimeStampInterval where the value is interpreted in minutes and multiplied by 60. On 2026-03-26, initial Procmon trigger attempts on Win25H2Clean (`perfmon /rel`, a bounded WER crash path, and WMI reliability queries) still did not produce live reads for the policy or fallback values inside the capture window. Follow-up work the same day narrowed the live path further: our Ghidra export on diagtrack.dll found the TimeStampInterval string, a DiagTrack service-restart Procmon pass showed an adjacent read on HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Reliability\PBR\LastSuccessfulRefreshTime, and bounded WER QueueReporting plus Problem Reports UI passes still did not show direct reads on TimeStampEnabled or TimeStampInterval. A later hidden structured Procmon pass then set the policy gate plus both interval variants together, ran DiagTrack restart, Consolidator, QueueReporting, perfmon /rel, and the Problem Reports UI in one capture window, and still surfaced only the same adjacent Reliability\PBR read on svchost.exe. A separate address-seeded Ghidra pass then re-opened the unresolved blocks from the first export at 18038fce0 and 18026bfc0, forced temporary function boundaries, and wrote both the match markdown and structured evidence.json back into the repo. Those forced decompiles are noisy, but they keep the unresolved code blocks in evidence instead of dropping them as `<no function>` dead ends. Between the official policy mapping and the decompiled interval logic, the record is now strong enough to treat the app's gate-plus-cap pair as an app-ready policy mapping even though the live 25H2 Procmon pass still lands on the adjacent Reliability\PBR branch instead of the exact target values.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: No explicit policy is set; the runtime can fall back to the current-version Reliability path if one is present.

allowed_values.label: Timestamping disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The decompiled function returns 0 immediately when TimeStampEnabled is 0.

allowed_values.label: Timestamping enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The decompiled function continues to read TimeStampInterval when TimeStampEnabled is non-zero.

targets.notes: TimeStampInterval is the companion cadence value. The policy path is read first; the current-version Reliability path is the fallback.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: No explicit interval policy is set; the runtime can fall back to the current-version Reliability path if one is present.

allowed_values.label: Maximum documented interval
allowed_values.meaning: The decompiled function caps the policy interval at 86400 seconds, which the repo note describes as 24h.

targets.notes: The policy-path interval is capped at 24h. The fallback current-version path stores minutes and the decompiled code multiplies by 60 before applying the same cap.

app_current_implementation.notes: The app now writes the documented policy gate plus the companion 24-hour interval cap used by this record.

windows_defaults.label: Windows managed default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Systems where this dataset has not yet published an explicit Reliability timestamp policy

states.rationale: Leave the policy unset and let Windows use its normal reliability timestamp behavior.

windows_defaults.notes: Microsoft documents the TimeStampEnabled policy path directly. The decompiled function then explains the companion interval and the current-version fallback path.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app policy
intended_for: Systems that need persistent shutdown timestamping
avoid_for: Systems where disk power-down behavior must stay untouched

states.rationale: Use the same 24-hour cap tracked in the decompiled reader and the current app mapping.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the app's current persistent timestamp policy pair with the documented gate enabled.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want to leave reliability timestamping fully Windows-managed.
tradeoffs: Keeps persistent shutdown timestamping enabled through the documented policy gate.
The companion interval still comes from decompiled runtime behavior instead of a separate Microsoft registry mapping.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: Policy CSP - ADMX_Reliability / EE_EnablePersistentTimeStamp
evidence.summary: Microsoft maps Enable Persistent Time Stamp directly to Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Reliability\TimeStampEnabled and documents the enabled, disabled, and not-configured behavior, including that the schedule is controlled by Timestamp Interval.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for reliability timestamping
evidence.summary: The repo note describes TimeStampEnabled / TimeStampInterval and documents the Reliability registry path used by the timestamping behavior.

evidence.title: Decompiled OsEventsTimestampInterval read path
evidence.summary: The decompiled function reads TimeStampEnabled from the policy Reliability path first, returns 0 when it is disabled, then reads TimeStampInterval and caps it at 86400 seconds before falling back to the current-version Reliability path.

evidence.title: Our Ghidra decompilation - diagtrack.dll reliability string/xref export
evidence.summary: Our 25H2 Ghidra exports on diagtrack.dll now include the original string/xref lead plus an address-seeded fallback pass for the unresolved blocks at 18038fce0 and 18026bfc0, which keeps the current-build DiagTrack path in repo evidence even though the exact registry read is still not visible in Procmon.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Procmon trigger attempts for Reliability timestamp reads
evidence.summary: Procmon passes around `perfmon /rel`, a bounded WER crash path, and WMI reliability queries all produced captures but did not show reads on the TimeStampEnabled or TimeStampInterval policy/fallback paths during the tested windows.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean Reliability follow-up - DiagTrack and WER queue probes
evidence.summary: A DiagTrack service-restart Procmon pass showed an adjacent Reliability\PBR read, and a later hidden structured probe that bundled DiagTrack restart, Consolidator, QueueReporting, perfmon /rel, and Problem Reports UI still produced only the same adjacent svchost.exe Reliability\PBR read instead of direct reads on TimeStampEnabled or TimeStampInterval.

decision.why: Microsoft now closes the main contract gap by publishing the exact TimeStampEnabled policy path and enable/disable semantics. The decompiled reader still supplies the companion TimeStampInterval logic and 24-hour cap, and the 25H2 follow-up work keeps the current-build DiagTrack lead in repo evidence. That is enough to treat the app's gate-plus-cap pair as an app-ready policy mapping even though the live Procmon pass still narrows to an adjacent Reliability branch instead of the exact target values.

open_questions: Should the companion TimeStampInterval cadence be split into its own record?
Should the app ever let users change the companion interval instead of keeping the 24-hour cap fixed?
Which live reliability or WER code path on 25H2 still issues the runtime reads seen in the decompiled function?
Is the 25H2 live path limited to adjacent DiagTrack Reliability branches while TimeStampEnabled stays historical or dormant?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: EE_EnablePersistentTimeStamp -> Registry Key Name Software\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows NT\\Reliability, Registry Value Name TimeStampEnabled. If you enable this policy setting, you are able to specify how often the Persistent System Timestamp is refreshed and subsequently written to the disk. You can specify the Timestamp Interval in seconds.
validation_proof.notes: The Microsoft policy page now documents the gate path directly. The decompiled function still confirms the gate behavior, the 24-hour cap on the companion interval, and the current-version fallback path. Our 25H2 follow-up work also found TimeStampInterval in diagtrack.dll, re-opened the unresolved DiagTrack blocks with an address-seeded Ghidra fallback pass, and captured the same adjacent Reliability\PBR read in both the DiagTrack restart pass and the later hidden structured Procmon pass.
